extracted_data.description: Rabbi Yosef Yitzchak Schneersohn (the Rayatz) is born in Lubavitch. At his circumcision, his grandfather predicts he will grow up to be a Rebbe.
Czar Alexander II of Russia is assassinated. Alexander III rolls back reforms and increases oppression of Russian Jews.
Nechama Dina Schneersohn, the future wife of the Rayatz, is born in Kishinev.
Czar Alexander III institutes the May Laws, heavily restricting Jewish residency, economic growth, and education.
The early Russian Zionist group BILU sends its first settlers to Eretz Yisrael.
Rabbi Shmuel implies to his wife that his passing is imminent, telling her he will 'rise up thirty-two steps in a spiritual way'.
Three days after Yom Kippur, Rabbi Shmuel passes away in Lubavitch at age forty-eight.
Chabad elders convene and implore the Rebbe's sons to assume leadership. They initially refuse but consent to recite maamarim privately.
Two-year-old Yosef Yitzchak watches his uncle Mendel's wedding. During mourning, his mother brings him to his father's study where he is moved by intense praying.
The Rashab returns to Lubavitch after seeking medical treatment, so changed that young Yosef Yitzchak doesn't recognize him.
At age four, Yosef Yitzchak begins formal education under Rabbi Yekusiel Aizik.
Rabbi Shmuel Betzalel Sheftel (the Rashbatz), future teacher of the Rayatz, relocates to Bulharkov.

extracted_data.title_en: Move to Yalta for Medical Treatment
extracted_data.description: Five-year-old Yosef Yitzchak travels with parents to Yalta for six months, taught by Rabbi Schneur Zalman Slonim.

extractions.passage: Move to Yalta for Medical Treatment

extracted_data.description: During a large crowded dinner where the Rashab delivers a maamar, Yosef Yitzchak notices his mother crying and informs his father.
At age seven, Yosef Yitzchak begins studying with Rabbi Shimshon, memorizing two orders of Mishnah.
The Rashab and wife frequently seek medical treatment. Yosef Yitzchak finds solace with elderly Chasidim and begins recording chasidic lore.
The Rashab writes an ethical will at age twenty-seven outlining how his wife should educate their son if he were to pass away.
Samuel Polyakov, the 'rail king of Russia,' passes away.
The Rashab and wife return to Lubavitch. Nine-year-old Yosef Yitzchak feels renewed maturity and begins facing his father during maamarim.
The Rashab officially begins to receive Chasidim for yechidut (private meetings).

extracted_data.title_en: Rashab Spends Full Year at Home
extracted_data.description: For the first time in years, the Rashab spends the entire year at home in Lubavitch.

extractions.passage: Rashab Spends Full Year at Home

extracted_data.title_en: Yosef Yitzchak's Prayer at the Ohel
extracted_data.description: The ten-year-old sneaks out in a snowstorm to the Ohel to pray for his father's recovery. The fever miraculously breaks.

extractions.passage: Yosef Yitzchak's Prayer at the Ohel

extracted_data.description: The Rashab contracts pneumonia and is dangerously ill for six weeks, asking Yosef Yitzchak to stay by his side.
During the yarmarka, Yosef Yitzchak operates a microloan fund. After defending a butcher, he is wrongfully arrested but released.
Yosef Yitzchak establishes a loan fund for local merchants.
The Rashab formally begins studying chasidic philosophy with Yosef Yitzchak.
Max Lilienthal, German Enlightenment activist who attempted to secularize Jewish education in Russia, passes away.
Yosef Yitzchak celebrates his bar mitzvah, memorizing three maamarim and reciting the third by heart at a gathering.
The Rashab fully accepts the position of Rebbe, taking his father's seat near the holy ark after eleven years.
The Rashab appoints Rabbi Shmuel Betzalel Sheftel as Yosef Yitzchak's dedicated teacher.
Rabbi Zalman Aharon (the Raza) relocates from Lubavitch to Vitebsk, deferring leadership to his younger brother.
Czar Alexander III dies and is succeeded by Nicholas II, who continues oppressive anti-Jewish policies.
The Rashab travels to Bolivke, dedicating time to his fifteen-year-old son's development.
The Rashab takes Yosef Yitzchak to the Ohel, transferring responsibility of working for the public good.
From that day forward, Yosef Yitzchak formally begins community work as his father's personal secretary.

extracted_data.title_en: Conference of Jewish Leaders in Kovno
extracted_data.description: At just fifteen, the Rayatz is dispatched to Kovno to represent his father at a conference of Jewish religious leaders.

extractions.passage: Conference of Jewish Leaders in Kovno

extracted_data.title_en: Engagement of the Rayatz and Nechama Dina
extracted_data.description: The Rayatz becomes engaged to Nechama Dina Schneersohn in Bolivke.

extractions.passage: Engagement of the Rayatz and Nechama Dina

extracted_data.description: The Rashab falls severely ill and travels to Moscow, receiving a bleak prognosis but recovers and lives another twenty-four years.
Enlightenment activist Mordechai Ben Hillel visits and opens a secular school with 130 students despite the Rashab's opposition.

extracted_data.title_en: Wedding of the Rayatz and Nechama Dina
extracted_data.description: The chuppah takes place on Friday afternoon in the courtyard of the main synagogue in Lubavitch.

extractions.passage: Wedding of the Rayatz and Nechama Dina

extracted_data.description: At the wedding reception, the Rashab announces his revolutionary plan to open a yeshivah integrating Talmud and chasidic philosophy.
Studies officially begin for the first group of yeshivah students under Rabbi Shmuel Gronem.
Yeshivah students return from Zembin for the High Holidays, impressing visiting Chasidim with their transformation.
Eighteen-year-old Yosef Yitzchak is appointed executive director of the yeshivah and begins fundraising.
The Rashab officially names the new institution and declares its students will be titled temimim.
The Rayatz visits Moscow, where Eliezer Polyakov shares a story about his brother Samuel receiving a blessing for wealth.
The Jewish Colonial Association pledges 1 million francs to support Enlightenment schools. The Rashab organizes opposition.
The Rashab compares students to King David's soldiers, charging them with disseminating Chasidism to the broader world.

extracted_data.title_en: Successful Lobbying Against Second JCA Allocation
extracted_data.description: The Rashab and Rayatz organize lobbying that successfully blocks a second allocation of 600,000 francs.

extractions.passage: Successful Lobbying Against Second JCA Allocation

extracted_data.description: The Rayatz concludes his formal studies with the Rashbatz.
The Rayatz opens a textile mill in Dubrovno providing Shabbat-observant employment for 2000 Jews.
The Rayatz travels to St. Petersburg for communal matters.
The Rayatz is arrested for the second time due to accusations by teachers at the secular school in Lubavitch.
War breaks out between Japan and Russia, leading to enlistment of thousands of Jewish soldiers.
At age twenty-four, the Rayatz writes to relatives in Jerusalem asking them to search for rare books and manuscripts.
The Rashab launches an initiative to provide matzah and Passover supplies to Jewish soldiers on the Far East front.
The Rayatz participates in a covert meeting of prominent rabbinic leaders to create strategy against Russian pogroms.
The Rayatz is sent to Western Europe to persuade statesmen and bankers to apply economic pressure on Russia.
Members of Poalei Tzion march into the yeshivah. Days later, 200 return with weapons, throw stones, and shoot at the yeshivah.
Poalei Tzion raids the Rashab's summer home in Bolivke, brandishing revolvers while the Rayatz's daughters are present.
The Rayatz is investigated and arrested due to the riots in Lubavitch, though he proves they were not part of the yeshivah.
The Rayatz is arrested in St. Petersburg due to efforts to exempt rabbis from military service.
The Rashab successfully purchases Beit Romano, a large property in Hebron, laying foundation to establish Toras Emes.
The Rashab organizes a confidential meeting of allied rabbis to strategize for upcoming elections and a national rabbinic conference.
Twenty-five leading rabbis convene in Vilna. The Rayatz serves as secretary.
The Rayatz travels to Germany to confer with Jewish communal leaders.
The town of Lubavitch has 3,376 residents, including 2,325 Jews.
A national Rabbinical Conference is held in St. Petersburg. The Rayatz assists his father throughout.
The Rayatz is imprisoned in St. Petersburg for efforts to exempt young men from military service.

extracted_data.title_en: Passing of Rabbi Yitzchak Dovber of Liadi
extracted_data.description: Rabbi Yitzchak Dovber of Liadi passes away.

extractions.passage: Passing of Rabbi Yitzchak Dovber of Liadi

extracted_data.description: The Rashab sends a group of outstanding students, led by Rabbi Zalman Havlin, to establish Toras Emes in Hebron.
The Rayatz outlines the purpose of the Hebron yeshivah in a letter to Rabbi Havlin.
The Rayatz experiences the first of four arrests that would occur between 1911 and 1916.
World Agudath Israel is established at a conference in Katowice, Poland.
The Young Israel movement is founded.
A synagogue is established by the small Jewish community in Kobe, Japan.
The Beilis affair begins, accusing Mendel Beilis of ritual murder in Kiev.
The Rashab dispatches the Rayatz to enlist prominent attorney Oscar Gruzenberg for Beilis's defense.

extracted_data.title_en: Appointment of Rabbi Dr. Yosef Hertz
extracted_data.description: Rabbi Dr. Yosef Hertz is appointed the chief rabbi of Britain.

extractions.passage: Appointment of Rabbi Dr. Yosef Hertz

extracted_data.description: World War I breaks out, and Germany declares war on Russia.
The Rebbe Rayatz's grandmother, Rebbetzin Rivkah Schneersohn, passes away.
The religious Zionist organization Mizrachi is launched.
The American Joint Distribution Committee is established to help Jews overseas.
The Released Time program for religious instruction begins in Indiana.
Henry Morgenthau, the Jewish US ambassador to the Turkish Empire, is permitted to visit the Tomb of the Patriarchs.
Germany invades Russia.
The Rashab and Rayatz depart Lubavitch with their families due to the advancing German army, ending 102 years of Chabad in Lubavitch.
The Rayatz is imprisoned in St. Petersburg a second time regarding military exemptions.
Jewish community leaders in Kutaisi ask the Rashab for help, and he dispatches rabbis to establish a yeshivah for Sephardic Jews.
The Bolshevik Revolution takes place in Russia. Czar Nicholas II abdicates and is eventually executed, ending the Pale of Settlement.
The Yevesektzia (Jewish section of Communist Party) is established to combat religious tradition.
The Communist government establishes the Cheka secret police.
Simon Yakovshvili stops in Rostov for Rosh HaShanah, meets the Rashab, and decides to stay to study Torah.
Semyon Dimenstein advocates settling Jews in agricultural colonies during conferences.
Yeshivah Torah Vodaath is founded in Williamsburg, Brooklyn.
Grigory Lulov joins the Cheka secret police.
The Yevesektzia holds a conference in Moscow, establishing the First Jewish Educational Council which bans Hebrew and Bible from schools.

extracted_data.title_en: Founding of Hebrew Theological College Chicago
extracted_data.description: The Hebrew Theological College is founded in Chicago.

extractions.passage: Founding of Hebrew Theological College Chicago

extracted_data.description: Bolsheviks take control of Rostov, prompting the Rashab to curtail public activities.
The Rashab summons the Rayatz, predicting 'dark clouds' for 22 years under Communism, prophesying the regime's eventual collapse.
A farbrengen at the Rashab's home is interrupted by Bolshevik police; the officers leave after the Rashab delivers a maamar.
Shortly after Purim, the Rashab falls ill with typhoid.
The Rashab passes away in Rostov at the age of 59.
The Rayatz accepts the position of the Sixth Lubavitcher Rebbe, reciting a maamar to Chasidim.
The Rayatz is arrested by Cheka officers, interrogated about Judaism, and subsequently released.
Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim relocates from Kremenchuk to Rostov due to extreme famine.
A typhus epidemic intensifies in Rostov, causing the Rebbe, his mother, and daughters to fall ill.
The Yevesektzia stages a show trial against the Lubavitch yeshivah in Rostov, demanding its closure.
The Rebbe's oldest daughter, Chana, marries Rabbi Shmaryahu Gurary in Rostov.
News arrives of a new branch of the yeshivah opening in Warsaw.
To escape famine and scrutiny, Yeshivah Tomchei Temimim relocates to Poltava.
The Rebbe establishes the Rabbinical Committee to advocate for religious rights under Soviet law.
The Ramash meets his future wife, Chaya Mushka, for the first time in Kislovodsk.
The Rebbe asks for volunteers to act as emissaries to establish secret places of Jewish study across the country.
In Moscow, the Rebbe formalizes a 'secret covenant' with nine trusted Chasidim, swearing an oath of self-sacrifice for Jewish education.
Facing imminent threat of arrest, the Rebbe is forced to flee Rostov and relocates to Leningrad.

extracted_data.title_en: Founding of Agudas Chassidei Chabad in US
extracted_data.description: Agudas Chassidei Chabad is established in the United States.

extractions.passage: Founding of Agudas Chassidei Chabad in US

extracted_data.description: Lev Gurewicz attempts to enlist the Rebbe to support a national conference. The Rebbe opposes it as a Yevesektzia-backed plot.
The Rebbe acquires a massive private library collection containing thousands of rare volumes.
A confidential JDC report reveals 15,000 children are being educated in secret schools.
A rabbinical conference occurs in Korosten. The Rebbe sends an emissary to steer the agenda toward tradition.
The Rebbe narrowly evades Yevesektzia agents on a train and in his hotel, saved by a sympathetic GPU official.
The Rebbe's secretary, Rabbi Elchonon Morozov, is arrested and jailed.
The Rebbe leads a fiery farbrengen, urging Chasidim to defy the government and be willing to cast themselves into flames.
Just after midnight, the Rebbe is arrested by GPU officers and taken to Shpalerka prison, sentenced to death by firing squad.
Massive international outcry forces the Soviets to commute his sentence to three-year exile in Kostroma.
The Rebbe is informed of his release from exile and returns to Leningrad two days later.
The Rebbe hides in Malakhovka outside Moscow while exit visas are secured.
The Rebbe celebrates his final holiday in Russia, surrounded by thousands of weeping Chasidim.
The Rebbe departs Russia by train with his historic library, arriving to a hero's welcome in Riga.
The Rebbe publishes his first maamar since leaving Russia, printed in Yiddish to broaden dissemination.
The 'Royal Wedding' of the Rebbe's daughter Chaya Mushka to the Ramash takes place at the yeshivah in Warsaw.
The Rebbe orchestrates an international campaign to ship 140,000 kilos of matzah into the Soviet Union.
The Rebbe embarks on a year-long journey, first traveling to Eretz Yisrael.
He visits Jerusalem, meets Chief Rabbis Kook and Sonnenfeld, visits Hebron and Me'arat HaMakhpelah.
The Rebbe arrives in New York Harbor to a crowd of thousands.
Celebrating the High Holidays in Brooklyn, he declares his transformative vision: 'America iz nisht aderesh'.
The Rebbe travels to Washington to meet with Supreme Court Justice Louis Brandeis about aiding Soviet Jews.
Sixteen Jewish leaders are arrested in Minsk for corresponding with the Rebbe.
The Rebbe visits St. Louis, Missouri, where thousands welcome him and he inspires a mikveh campaign.
The Rebbe meets with US President Herbert Hoover at the White House.
The Rebbe departs the US for Europe.
The Rebbe recovers in Marienbad, meeting with the Belzer and Gerrer Rebbes.
The Rebbe confronts members of a Communist youth club plotting to disrupt Simchat Torah.
The Rebbe visits Rakshik, Lithuania, to give local Chasidim an opportunity to visit him.
The Rebbe travels to Warsaw to survey the yeshivah and meets with students from Lodz and Vilnius.
The Rebbe writes a lengthy letter to the Ramash detailing Chabad's intellectual and spiritual focus.
The Rebbe celebrates the marriage of his youngest daughter Sheina to Rabbi Mendel Horenstein.
The Rebbe spends two months in Berlin for medical care, accompanied by the Ramash.

extracted_data.title_en: Ramash and Chaya Mushka Flee Berlin
extracted_data.description: Fleeing the Nazi threat, the Ramash and Chaya Mushka leave Berlin and resettle in Paris.

extractions.passage: Ramash and Chaya Mushka Flee Berlin

extracted_data.description: After securing Latvian citizenship, the Rebbe permanently relocates to Warsaw, Poland.
Rabbi Yitzchak Horowitz arrives in New York as the Rebbe's emissary to strengthen Chabad in America.
Rabbi Chaim Mordechai Aizek Hodakov is appointed chief inspector and begins reforming the curriculum.
Seeking treatment for illness, the Rebbe travels to Vienna where Dr. Max Gerson diagnoses multiple sclerosis.
Due to health needs, the Rebbe relocates permanently to Otwock, Poland.
The journal Hatomim is founded and the women's organization is created.
Latvian police arrest four Chabad Chasidim running the Rebbe's covert package-smuggling operation.
Simon Yakovshvili is arrested by secret police, tortured, and sentenced to death before commutation.
The Rebbe departs Austria for Paris just three days before Germany annexes Austria.
Students from the US begin enrolling in the Rebbe's yeshivah in Otwock.
The Rebbe's secretary is captured and executed by firing squad.
Germany invades Poland and World War II begins. Otwock is bombed.
The Rebbe and family are evacuated to Warsaw in a Latvian Embassy vehicle.
Admiral Wilhelm Canaris rescues the Rebbe and family; they arrive safely in Riga.
The US Consulate in Riga issues visas to the Rebbe and his entourage.
The Rebbe departs Riga by plane to Stockholm.
The Rebbe boards the SS Drottningholm in Gothenburg, Sweden.
The Drottningholm arrives in New York Harbor. The Rebbe famously declares 'America iz nisht aderesh'.
The day after arrival, the Rebbe announces the opening of Yeshivas Tomchei Temimim in America.
NY Governor signs statute approving the Released Time program. The Rebbe immediately harnesses this.
Agudas Chassidei Chabad purchases 770 Eastern Parkway as the Rebbe's permanent residence.
The Rebbe moves into his permanent headquarters at 770 Eastern Parkway in Crown Heights.

extracted_data.title_en: Ramash and Chaya Mushka Flee Paris
extracted_data.description: After fleeing Paris to Vichy, they board a ship in Lisbon.

extractions.passage: Ramash and Chaya Mushka Flee Paris

extracted_data.description: The Ramash and Chaya Mushka arrive safely in New York.
As Pearl Harbor is attacked, Rabbi Mottel Altein is traveling by train to establish a Hebrew school.
The Rebbe writes an anguished letter lamenting the silence regarding the Holocaust.
The Rebbe's youngest daughter is murdered in the Treblinka death camp.
The Rebbe's son-in-law is murdered in Treblinka.
A dedicated building is purchased for the growing Chabad yeshivah in Montreal.
The Ramash's father, Rabbi Levi Yitzchak Schneerson, passes away in exile in Kazakhstan.
The Rebbe suffers a heart attack after receiving a report on the Nazi massacre of Jews in Riga.
A Chabad relief office is established in Paris to assist refugees.
Chabad Chasidim begin secretly crossing the border from Lvov to Poland using forged Polish passports.
The Ramash travels to Paris to reunite with his mother after 20 years of separation.
A massive transport of 232 Chasidim successfully crosses the Russian border. The Rebbe senses their crossing spiritually.
The Great Escape ends when Soviet authorities arrest escape organizers in Lvov.
Following the establishment of the State of Israel, the Rebbe directs the founding of Kfar Chabad.
Israel holds its first elections. The Rebbe instructs Chasidim to participate and vote.
The Rebbe and his family are granted US citizenship at 770 Eastern Parkway.
The Rebbe meets with Yitzchak Levy to plan sending teachers to North Africa.
In days leading up to his passing, the Rebbe is highly active, dispatching letters globally and holding yechidut.
At 7:45 a.m. on Shabbat morning, the Rebbe suffers a final heart attack and passes away at 7:50 a.m.
Thousands crowd Eastern Parkway for the Rebbe's funeral. He is interred at Old Montefiore Cemetery in Queens.
